column6:
Playing team-based games can help you become a better team player. I think the primary way this happens is learning which information would be most useful to other people. This requires a degree of understanding of their situation and their abilities which you need to understand in order to give good, useful information which they can use to help the team.

This is a not always found in all team-based games. I think there needs to be an urgent objective (so not something like Stardew Valley, where basic cooperation can get you to the end of the game) and the players need to take the objective seriously, which is the downfall of most team games. If the players aren't taking the game very seriously, it can be hard to tell what their actions and interests will be, and if the information you are giving is actually useful.¬†

¬†

Other games with NPCs could help but it would be on a very basic level since there is an underlying script therefore a fairly straightforward solution/expectation for what will happen.

Though I normally game by myself, I think gaming has the capacity to make someone a better team player. The communication aspect alone is paramount to a well-functioning team; so practicing those skills through video games would probably help one realize what it takes to work together as a single entity.¬†

Yes.

I played OW competitively and semi pro for a while, T3/2 in 2021. That is a textbook team game, especially in the team/scrim environment. You have to work together, you have to be on the same page, or you just lose. Communication is key, you have to share information, idea, plans. Operating solo, independent from the team, will get you nothing. Things like scrimming and VOD reviews help to ensure everyone is on the same page. There is also that implicit peer pressure to put in the work to improve individually, as everyone else is as well.
Yes it does. I think about more than just myself (at least in Counter Strike). If the team is doing bad, then I am doing something wrong.

I think it depends on the type of games you are playing. If someone is playing for example MarioKart then the answer is probably no, now if you are specifically playing a mode where you are on a team then this answer changes. Using an example that has teams, like Phasmophobia, then you are probably quite good at working in a team. This game for example has everyone work together to find out what type of spirit is inhabiting the map you are on.¬†

For me personally, I think it has. Learning to work and collaborate with other people is incredibly important and can make or even break an important project. Each person brings something different to the table, and if all pieces aren't flowing well then everything else will break.
I would like to think so, it helps to be able to coherently work in a team to achieve a similar goal while not squabbling too much or just diddling about and not getting anything done.¬†

column6: It is my belief (cultivated over thousands of hours) that the popular coordinated online video games of today's world: League of Legends, Valorant, Fortnite, R6S, etc., do NOT provide help building team skills, at least not for most players. In order to succeed in games like these, you must adopt a strategy that is entirely agnostic to the sorts of people you are playing with. This is because each match will usually have an entirely different set of people than the last and learning/coordinating with the team must, as a result, be very simple. Teamwork is something that is built on understanding and effort and the short 20-30 minutes you have during a typical gaming session do not facilitate this. Now I said¬†most players because when you play with friends or collegiate team members for CSU, for example, you end up having more time to practice with them. Otherwise, longer-term games you play with friends (Minecraft, perhaps) are better for this. It's a nuanced question but I believe this is the main contour.

I am a better team player when it's people I know because I am able to criticize them if they make a mistake without it being a big deal. Otherwise, I can still be good in teams with randoms if we have good chemistry or we can all pull our own weight in the game.

I would say this is 50/50 dependent on the game and who I am playing with. Sometimes I have to carry my regular friends, but I have a couple people that I play with that are equal or better than me that I end up being more of a team player and less of trying to carry everyone else. When I play with people at my level in a competitive environment I would say I am a good team player.¬†

column6: Yes! I used to play a lot of Fortnite back when I was in high school with my friends where we would play as a group of 4 playing against another 24 teams of 4. Gameplan was simple, everyone would loot up with weapons and healables and we would either try to fight our way to a win or wait it out and fight it out in the final moments of the match. Either way, communication and teamwork was crucial. We communicated to each other if we found good weapons or healalbles and also when we spotted enemies we wanted to approach or were approaching us. Another thing it taught me is how important it is for everyone to be on the same page. Whenever one of my group members left to find loot alone or engage with another person it mostly lead to them being attacked by 1 or more enemies or the remaining 3 in my group being attacked by a group of 4 putting the rest of my team in a bad spot having to help the 1 getting attacked or being down a man when attacked. Moral of the story, it is important to communciate and have clear goals and objectives that everyone can adhere to as it allows for the group and its members to work more efficiently, clearly, and most of the time it results in better outcomes for both the team and its individuals.

column6: Very much so yes. One of my favorite games to play is a cooperative shooter called¬†GTFO, which is known for it's difficulty and for requiring smooth teamwork between a team of 4 players in order to have any hope of passing even the earliest levels. A good GTFO run requires clean communication, understanding of your teammate's abilities, loadout, personal strengths, personal weaknesses, and depending on your team and your playstyle, leadership. This game alone has made me so much better at communicating cleanly and taught me how to be more flexible in communication depending on what my team needs. When playing with my main four group that have the most experience in the game, I can shortcut communication, ask for "numbers" instead of asking specifically for ammo count or tool resources, and generally trust that my team will understand, but if I'm playing with a new player, I can be much more verbose and clear.¬†And while this isn't the only cooperative game I play, it's probably the best example of how cooperative gaming has made me a better team player.¬†

Highly recommend.¬†

column6: My gaming falls into three categories, usually. Cooperative gaming, which is when I play with my friends to achieve a common goal. It's helpful to know what you and your teammate's strengths are in this kind of game, and it's also helped me learn to accept help. Competitive gaming, which might seem against teamwork, has actually helped me get to know my friends better, and also made me better at tempering frustrations in order to focus more on the task at hand. Finally, story-based singleplayer games (my favorite), help me take time to myself to contemplate on what I can/can't do.

I believe that gaming makes you a better team player because it teaches you how to rely on others while also pulling your own weight. Especially in games with classes like Overwatch and Marvel Rivals, a proper balance between classes could be the difference between winning and losing. Games like these also teach you how to find your role without trying to take on too much of a load. For example, a tank player may drive forward toward an enemy team only to be surrounded and eliminated. If, however, the tank shields his healer and other fighters while moving toward the enemy team, all of his team members would benefit. Multiplayer games such as these teach you how to most effectively help your teammates and leave yourself in a position for them to help you as well.

Destiny 2 and World of Warcraft require analyzing a situation, dividing people by levels of skill and familiarity, assigning each to their areas of expertise or where they will do the least damage to the team, and then executing the task with perfect or near perfect timing. They also teach you patience with people less skilled than you, humility in recognizing your faults and how to address this disparity. I think these skills translate wonderfully to becoming a team player.

Most of the games I play are single player, so I don't think it's made much of an impact. For the games that are team efforts, I am really bad. The one trait I have seen improve is my patience, a very important team player skill.
Yes, the games I tend to play don't force you to work together, but in an environment that's meant to be open and creative I want to work with people. Gaming is a convenient tool for collaboration.

It does. It gives you many opportunities to communicate with others to achieve a goal. Games without goals allow you to think creatively. Sometimes it can also allow you to do things individually where others are not / cannot. Most team games require communication and team awareness. Playing to these skills in games allows you to win more often¬†

column5: Minecraft,Chess,Call of Duty,Super Smash Bros,Mortal Kombat
column6: Does my gaming make me a better team player, my honest answer is yes with a stipulation. I believe I can contribute well to a common goal when put in this situation, such as Safe Guard on CODuty or a shared Minecraft server's goals. However, most of the games I enjoy and frequent are not team games. I enjoy games where the outcome is solely based on your input IE chess, SSB, or MK. I believe in the right scenario when working towards a common goal I become a better team player in game and the real world.

Yes but one aspect causes a blind spot.

Gaming has taught me to quickly read team dynamics (and individual motivations) and adapt my interactions and even roles based on that. To me that is the motivation for me to play, searching for that commonality. I‚Äôve also learned the worst teammates aren't the least skilled players, they're the ones who work against the team (can be often skilled and highly experienced), or choose to be solo and make unilateral choices that¬† jeopardize the team (someone revives them) or blames others for their own unilateral choices.¬† I‚Äôve seen the same patterns in coding projects with developers who ignore requirements or disappear when needed most and even re-emerge wanting to be ‚Äòlate-leaders‚Äô after their poor choices caused the derailment. If there is a player with no skill but shows eagerness and is socially engaged, the game changes for me where I‚Äôm playing the game at their pace (still wanting to win) in order to make it enjoyable. I‚Äôm not a fan of people demanding others to do their play style, and that‚Äôs a behavior that snowballs into something worse. All of these aspects are in gaming and human nature.

What gaming really taught me is adaptability and understanding motivations (spotting red flags too like what they say doesn‚Äôt align with their actions). The online games I play can be between 45 minutes to 1 hour for a single match, so‚Ä¶ there is a lot of adaptability required to make the experience enjoyable yet move forward. I naturally lean toward leadership (because I create momentum, proactive communicator), but I've learned when to step back and support someone if I feel it‚Äôll help the project. The key is figuring out what each person wants and whether accommodating that moves the project forward or creates problems. I will say this, the difference between gaming and being a team player for coding is there is no schadenfreude (toxic pleasure) when seeing a very negative teammate fail. You have to have the project work, and in an occupation there is a thing called leadership traps, where if it happens there isn‚Äôt really a clear template on how to handle it since failure shouldn‚Äôt be an option (at least in my opinion) or if word gets out that it‚Äôs reach this point no one will look good.
